claims.claim_text: The anecdote claims a specific $312 monthly OpenAI bill.
claims.span_hint: section: The $600/Year Mistake That Changed Everything
claims.anchor_text: Three hundred and twelve dollars. In a single month. All from OpenAI.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For some developers, an unexpectedly high monthly API bill is the moment they start looking at local models.
claims.rationale: This is a named anecdote with fake-precise pricing and no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The anecdote claims the same prompts had cost $50 the previous day and were now completely free.
claims.span_hint: section: The $600/Year Mistake That Changed Everything
claims.anchor_text: He ran the same prompts that had cost him fifty dollars the day before—completely free.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Running similar prompts locally removes per-request API fees, though hardware and electricity still have real costs.
claims.rationale: The exact dollar figure and day-over-day comparison are unsourced anecdotal specifics.

claims.claim_text: The anecdote claims Marcus used local models for 80% of his work and cut his bill from $300 to $15.
claims.span_hint: section: The $600/Year Mistake That Changed Everything
claims.anchor_text: His monthly API bill dropped from $300 to $15.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A hybrid workflow can reduce API spending substantially by moving routine tasks to local models and reserving paid APIs for harder problems.
claims.rationale: This is a specific unsourced savings claim attached to a named anecdote.

claims.claim_text: The module attributes a verbatim quote to Marcus Chen at PyCon 2025.
claims.span_hint: section: The $600/Year Mistake That Changed Everything
claims.suggested_rewrite: One useful way to think about local models is as routine-task assistants in a hybrid workflow, rather than full replacements for the strongest hosted models.
claims.rationale: This is an attributed real-world quote/event with no citable source.

claims.claim_text: The anecdote claims $600/year in savings funded a new GPU.
claims.span_hint: section: The $600/Year Mistake That Changed Everything
claims.anchor_text: That $600/year in savings? It funded Marcus's new GPU, which made his local models even faster.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For frequent users, lower API spend can offset some of the cost of local hardware over time.
claims.rationale: This is another fake-precise continuation of the same unsourced anecdote.

claims.claim_text: Running models locally means $0/month AI coding.
claims.span_hint: section: What You'll Be Able to Do
claims.anchor_text: Running models locally means **$0/month** AI coding, complete privacy, and offline capability.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Running models locally can eliminate per-token API fees for many coding tasks, while also improving privacy and offline availability.
claims.rationale: The zero-dollar claim is an illustrative oversimplification because local use still has hardware and power costs.

claims.claim_text: The module claims a precise $600-1,200/year vs $60-120/year comparison and a 10x reduction.
claims.span_hint: section: The Local Models Revolution
claims.suggested_rewrite: **The difference**: moving routine work to local models can cut API spend dramatically for heavy users.
claims.rationale: These are illustrative cost scenarios, not source-backed annual totals.

claims.claim_text: The module says Meta released Llama 4 in 2024 and that it changed everything.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Open Source AI Boom
claims.suggested_rewrite: **2024-2025**: newer open models from several labs improved local coding performance noticeably.
claims.rationale: This bundles a dated release claim with editorialized impact language and no cited primary source.

claims.claim_text: The module says DeepSeek Coder beats GPT-3.5 on coding tasks.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Open Source AI Boom
claims.anchor_text: DeepSeek Coder beats GPT-3.5 on coding tasks
claims.suggested_rewrite: Newer open code models began closing the gap on older hosted baselines in several coding evaluations.
claims.rationale: The exact comparative claim needs a benchmark source and specific model/version pairing.

claims.claim_text: The module says Qwen 2.5-Coder rivals commercial models.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Open Source AI Boom
claims.suggested_rewrite: Qwen2.5-Coder 32B is presented by its authors as competitive with strong proprietary coding models on some benchmarks.
claims.rationale: The generic claim is too broad as written and needs a size-specific, sourceable formulation.

claims.claim_text: DeepSeek R1 competes with o1 on reasoning.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Open Source AI Boom
claims.rationale: The DeepSeek-R1 model card explicitly says it achieves performance comparable to OpenAI-o1 across math, code, and reasoning tasks.

claims.claim_text: The module says Qwen 2.5-Coder:32B rivals gpt-5 on code.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Open Source AI Boom
claims.suggested_rewrite: Qwen2.5-Coder 32B is presented by its authors as matching GPT-4o on coding tasks.
claims.rationale: The available primary source supports a GPT-4o comparison, not this GPT-5 claim.

claims.claim_text: DeepSeek Coder V2 is a specialized coding model that rivals gpt-5 on benchmarks.
claims.span_hint: section: The Local Model Landscape / DeepSeek Coder V2
claims.anchor_text: Specialized coding model that rivals gpt-5 on benchmarks
claims.suggested_rewrite: Specialized coding model whose authors compare it with leading proprietary models on some coding benchmarks.
claims.rationale: The specific GPT-5 comparison is not backed by the available primary source.

claims.claim_text: DeepSeek Coder V2 supports 338 programming languages.
claims.span_hint: section: The Local Model Landscape / DeepSeek Coder V2
claims.rationale: The model card explicitly states support expanded to 338 programming languages.

claims.claim_text: DeepSeek Coder V2 is available in 16B and 236B variants.
claims.span_hint: section: The Local Model Landscape / DeepSeek Coder V2
claims.rationale: The model card lists 16B and 236B releases.

claims.claim_text: DeepSeek Coder V2 beats GPT-3.5-Turbo and rivals gpt-5 on HumanEval.
claims.span_hint: section: The Local Model Landscape / DeepSeek Coder V2
claims.anchor_text: **Benchmarks**: Beats GPT-3.5-Turbo, rivals gpt-5 on HumanEval
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Benchmarks**: presented as competitive with strong proprietary coding baselines in the model card.
claims.rationale: The exact comparative benchmark wording is not backed by an allowlisted primary source passage.

claims.claim_text: CodeLlama is Meta's specialized coding version of Llama 4.
claims.span_hint: section: The Local Model Landscape / CodeLlama
claims.anchor_text: Meta's specialized coding version of Llama 4
claims.suggested_rewrite: Meta's coding-focused model family built on top of Llama 2.
claims.rationale: The official Code Llama model card describes it as built on Llama 2, not Llama 4.

claims.claim_text: Codestral is a 22B coding-specialized model.
claims.span_hint: section: The Local Model Landscape / Codestral
claims.rationale: Mistral's model page identifies Codestral as a 22B coding model.

claims.claim_text: Phi-3.5 is a 3.8B-parameter model.
claims.span_hint: section: The Local Model Landscape / Phi-3.5
claims.rationale: Microsoft's model card states Phi-3.5 mini has 3.8B parameters.

claims.claim_text: Apple released the M1 chip in November 2020 and everything changed for local AI.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Apple Silicon Revolution
claims.anchor_text: Then Apple released the **M1 chip** in November 2020. Everything changed.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Apple Silicon made local inference much more practical on consumer laptops.
claims.rationale: This combines a dated release event with sweeping unsourced impact language.

claims.claim_text: A $999 MacBook Air can now run models that would have cost $10,000+ in GPU hardware three years earlier.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Apple Silicon Revolution
claims.anchor_text: A $999 MacBook Air can now run models that would have cost $10,000+ in GPU hardware just 3 years ago.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Modern consumer laptops can run useful local models that previously required much more specialized hardware.
claims.rationale: This is false precision around historical hardware equivalence and pricing.

claims.claim_text: Any Mac with an M-series chip can run production-quality AI models locally.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? The Apple Silicon Revolution
claims.anchor_text: If you have any Mac with M-series chip (M1, M2, M3, M4), you can run production-quality AI models locally.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Many M-series Macs can run useful local models, but the practical model size and speed still depend on RAM, storage, and workload.
claims.rationale: The claim is too broad and absolute for the wide range of M-series hardware.

claims.claim_text: Ollama was created by Jeffrey Morgan and Michael Chiang in 2023.
claims.span_hint: section: Hands-On / What is Ollama? / Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: Ollama was created by Jeffrey Morgan and Michael Chiang in 2023.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Ollama emerged in 2023 to make running local models much easier for developers.
claims.rationale: The named-founder claim needs a reliable primary source that is not provided here.

claims.claim_text: Within a year, Ollama had over 500,000 users and became the de facto standard for running local models.
claims.span_hint: section: Hands-On / What is Ollama? / Did You Know?
claims.anchor_text: Within a year, Ollama had over 500,000 users and became the de facto standard for running local models.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Ollama quickly became one of the most widely used tools for running local models.
claims.rationale: The precise user count and market-position claim are unsourced.

claims.claim_text: Aider can work with almost any LLM, including local models.
claims.span_hint: section: Hands-On / What is Aider?
claims.rationale: Aider's README says it can connect to almost any LLM, including local models.

claims.claim_text: Aider supports `.aider.conf.yml` with settings such as `model`, `auto-commits`, `show-diffs`, and `pretty`.
claims.span_hint: section: Hands-On / Aider Configuration File
claims.rationale: The sample config documents these keys and defaults.

claims.claim_text: The Continue setup tells readers to edit `config.json` as if that were the current default path.
claims.span_hint: section: Hands-On / Configuring for Local Models
claims.suggested_rewrite: Edit Continue's current configuration file (prefer `config.yaml`; use legacy `config.json` only if your installed version still expects it):
claims.rationale: The module presents legacy configuration guidance as the default without version qualification.

claims.claim_text: Gemini Flash is free tier and costs $0 in this workflow.
claims.span_hint: section: Cost Optimization / Your Hybrid Setup
claims.suggested_rewrite: A free-tier API model may cover some overflow work, but availability and limits change over time.
claims.rationale: Free-tier availability is a time-sensitive vendor pricing claim.

claims.claim_text: The hybrid setup will total about $5/month versus $50-100 for API-only use.
claims.span_hint: section: Cost Optimization / Your Hybrid Setup
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Total**: a hybrid setup can reduce monthly API spend substantially compared with relying on paid APIs for everything.
claims.rationale: These are teaching numbers, not stable published prices.

claims.claim_text: HumanEval table row: gpt-5 scores 67% and costs $30 per 1M tokens.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Benchmarks / HumanEval table
claims.suggested_rewrite: | GPT-5 | Strong proprietary benchmark performer | Paid API pricing varies by model and date | Fast |
claims.rationale: The exact score and price snapshot are unsourced and time-sensitive.

claims.claim_text: HumanEval table row: Claude 3.5 Sonnet scores 64% and costs $15 per 1M tokens.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Benchmarks / HumanEval table
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Claude-class API model | Strong proprietary benchmark performer | Paid API pricing varies by model and date | Fast |
claims.rationale: The row mixes exact benchmark and pricing claims without a current primary source.

claims.claim_text: HumanEval table row: DeepSeek Coder V2 16B scores 62%.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Benchmarks / HumanEval table
claims.suggested_rewrite: | DeepSeek Coder V2 16B | Strong open-model coding performance | No per-token API fee when run locally | Medium |
claims.rationale: The exact percentage and zero-cost framing are not appropriately sourced here.

claims.claim_text: HumanEval table row: Qwen 2.5-Coder 32B scores 61%.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Benchmarks / HumanEval table
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Qwen2.5-Coder 32B | Strong open-model coding performance | No per-token API fee when run locally | Medium |
claims.rationale: The exact benchmark number is unsupported in the module.

claims.claim_text: HumanEval table row: CodeLlama 34B scores 54%.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Benchmarks / HumanEval table
claims.suggested_rewrite: | CodeLlama 34B | Older open coding baseline | No per-token API fee when run locally | Medium |
claims.rationale: This exact score needs a benchmark citation that is not provided.

claims.claim_text: HumanEval table row: Gemini Flash 2.5 scores 71% and is free tier $0.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Benchmarks / HumanEval table
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Gemini Flash-class API model | Strong hosted benchmark performer | Pricing and free-tier access vary over time | Very Fast |
claims.rationale: Both the exact score and the free-tier price claim are unstable as written.

claims.claim_text: Speed table row: gpt-5 generates a 100-line Python function in 2-3 seconds.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Benchmarks / Speed Comparison
claims.suggested_rewrite: | GPT-5 (API) | Often faster than local inference, depending on network and service load | High |
claims.rationale: The timing claim is environment-specific and uncited.

claims.claim_text: Speed table row: Qwen 2.5-Coder 7B takes 8-12 seconds.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Benchmarks / Speed Comparison
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Qwen2.5-Coder 7B (local) | Usually slower than a hosted API on the same task, but often acceptable for daily use | Good |
claims.rationale: The timing is machine-specific and should not be presented as a fixed fact.

claims.claim_text: Speed table row: DeepSeek V2 16B takes 15-20 seconds.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Benchmarks / Speed Comparison
claims.suggested_rewrite: | DeepSeek 16B-class local model | Typically slower than smaller local models, but often stronger on harder tasks | High |
claims.rationale: The exact latency is hardware- and runtime-dependent.

claims.claim_text: Speed table row: Phi-3.5 3.8B takes 5-8 seconds.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Benchmarks / Speed Comparison
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Phi-3.5 3.8B (local) | Usually faster than larger local models, with more limited capability | Moderate |
claims.rationale: This exact performance figure is not portable across machines.

claims.claim_text: Local models are 90-95% as good as API, at 0% of the cost.
claims.span_hint: section: Performance Benchmarks / Insight line
claims.anchor_text: **Insight**: Local models are 90-95% as good as API, at 0% of the cost!
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Insight**: local models are often good enough for many coding tasks, especially when you do not need the strongest reasoning or the newest features.
claims.rationale: The percentage and absolute cost claim are unsourced and overly absolute.

claims.claim_text: A 236B model needs 200+ GB RAM or GPU.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes / Mistake #1
claims.anchor_text: 236B model needs 200+ GB RAM or GPU
claims.suggested_rewrite: A 236B model is impractical on a 16GB laptop and typically requires far more memory or specialized hardware than consumer laptops provide.
claims.rationale: The exact memory threshold depends on quantization and runtime choices.

claims.claim_text: The rule of thumb says model params × 2 equals GB needed.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes / Mistake #1
claims.anchor_text: Match model size to your RAM (model params × 2 = GB needed)
claims.suggested_rewrite: Match model size to your hardware using the runtime's published model size and real-world tests, because memory needs vary with quantization, context length, and offloading.
claims.rationale: This formula is too simplistic to present as a factual rule.

claims.claim_text: The module promises real cost savings of $200-500/year.
claims.span_hint: section: Next Steps / You've learned
claims.suggested_rewrite: Potential cost savings from shifting routine work to local models
claims.rationale: This is an illustrative savings range rather than a stable published figure.

further_reading.title: Ollama README
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream entry point for installing Ollama and understanding how local model runtimes work.

further_reading.title: Aider README
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream reference for using Aider with cloud and local models.

further_reading.title: DeepSeek-R1 model card
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful primary source for current open reasoning-model capabilities and distilled local variants.

notes: Quiz items and exercise steps were excluded per instructions. Code-block-only commands were generally excluded unless the surrounding prose made an independent factual claim. Tabular benchmark rows were included because they contain precise statistical claims that need citation or rewrite. Supported claims carry their primary source in `proposed_url`; rewrite dispositions intentionally remove unsourced precision instead of forcing weak citations.
